question: What makes up living soil?
title: What Makes Up Living Soil? Inside the Web of Life Beneath Your Feet
metaDescription: Living soil isn't dirt, it's a thriving ecosystem of bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and more. Scotty breaks down the soil food web and why it's the foundation of everything you grow.
content:
Hey everybody. People ask me this one all the time. What is living soil, and how do you know if yours is alive?

Honest answer: pick up a handful of genuinely healthy soil and compare it to a handful of dirt. You'll feel the difference before I even explain it.

Dirt is mineral particles. Sand, silt, clay. It holds moisture at different rates, it compacts under weight, and on its own it doesn't do much. You can force plants to grow in it with enough synthetic fertilizer, but you're doing all the chemistry yourself. The soil isn't helping you.

Living soil is a different animal. Grab a scoop of it and it holds together but crumbles cleanly, it doesn't smear or fall apart into powder. That structure comes from what soil scientists call aggregates, little clumps of mineral particles that are basically glued together by biology. Fungal hyphae thread through them. Bacterial biofilms coat them. You're holding biological activity in your hand.

That's the starting point.

---

## The Organisms That Make Soil Live

The USDA NRCS Soil Biology Primer describes the soil food web as a network of plants, bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes, and larger predatory species. One of the most complex ecosystems on Earth, and it's right under your feet.

Here are the main players.

**Bacteria** are the smallest and they are everywhere. One teaspoon of healthy soil can hold hundreds of millions to billions of individual bacteria, thousands of species. They decompose fresh organic matter. Some fix atmospheric nitrogen, pulling N2 gas out of the air and converting it into a form your plants can actually use. They produce enzymes that break complex organic compounds into simpler ones. They are basically the starting engine for most nutrient cycles in your soil.

**Fungi** often dominate the biomass in healthy soil even though their individual numbers are lower than bacteria. Those thin white threads running through good compost or forest duff, that's fungal hyphae. They extend through your soil like a fiber-optic network. Mycorrhizal fungi go further, they form direct partnerships with plant roots, extending the root system by orders of magnitude and trading minerals (especially phosphorus) for plant sugars. One teaspoon of healthy forest soil can hold several miles of fungal hyphae.

**Protozoa** are single-celled organisms that graze on bacteria. Sounds minor, but it matters a lot. When a protozoan eats bacteria, it takes in more nitrogen than it needs and excretes the rest as plant-available ammonium. This is called the microbial loop. It's one of the main ways nitrogen gets to your plant roots. Healthy protozoa populations mean healthy nitrogen cycling.

**Nematodes** are microscopic roundworms in predator-prey relationships all the way through the food web. Bacterial-feeding nematodes eat bacteria. Fungal-feeding nematodes eat hyphae. Predatory nematodes eat other nematodes and protozoa. Every predation event releases nutrients that cycle back to plants.

**Earthworms and arthropods** are the ones you can actually see. Earthworms pull organic matter down into the soil, mix it with mineral particles, and produce castings that are extraordinarily nutrient-rich. A good earthworm population is one of the best indicators of soil health you've got without a microscope. Arthropods, mites, springtails, beetles, shred organic matter into smaller pieces, making it accessible to the bacterial and fungal communities below.

## How to Tell If Your Soil Is Living

Here's what I actually look for in the field.

**Aggregates.** Grab a handful of moist soil. If it crumbles into small irregular clumps that hold their shape rather than smearing or falling apart, biology is holding those particles together. USDA NRCS flags aggregate formation as a key biological indicator of soil health. Good aggregates mean good pore structure, water infiltrates, roots penetrate, air moves through.

**Earthworms.** Dig down six inches in genuinely living soil and you should hit earthworms. Healthy systems have dozens per square foot. Dig down and find nothing, no worms, no insects, and that soil is depleted.

**Fungal hyphae.** Break apart a chunk of good soil or partially decomposed mulch. You should see white or off-white threads running through it. That's your fungal network, intact and active.

**The smell.** Living soil smells like living soil. That rich, earthy, almost sweet smell, it's geosmin produced by actinomycetes, and it's one of the most reliable sensory indicators of biological activity there is. Depleted soil smells like nothing. Or like dust.

## The Decay Cycle and Why It Matters

Albert Howard figured something out at Indore in the 1930s that the rest of agriculture has been slowly catching up to ever since. The decay cycle, the process by which organic matter breaks down and rebuilds into stable humus, is not a side process. It's the central process. It's what makes soil alive, what makes plants healthy, what makes nutrients available without synthetic inputs.

Gabe Brown builds on this directly. You can't have a healthy farm without a healthy soil food web. You can't have a healthy soil food web without organic matter. And you can't have organic matter without something continuously covering and feeding your soil.

The life in your soil gets fed two ways: the organic matter you add from the surface, mulch, compost, cover crop residue, and the sugars that plant roots exude into the rhizosphere to feed specific microbial communities. That second one is easy to miss. A living plant is actively farming its own microbial community around its roots. It produces and releases specific sugars to attract the organisms it needs for nutrient exchange. This relationship is 400 million years old and it's still running in every living root zone on Earth.

When you kill the soil biology, tillage, synthetic inputs that bypass the microbial pathway, bare soil baking in the sun, you break that relationship. What you have left is dirt with chemistry applied to it.

Living soil is what y'all are building when you compost, when you mulch, when you grow cover crops, when you minimize disturbance. Every one of those practices feeds the web. And the web feeds everything above it.

That's what makes soil live.

---

## Sources

1. USDA NRCS, Soil Biology Primer: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/resources/education-and-teaching-materials/soil-biology-primer
2. USDA NRCS, Healthy Soils Are Full of Life: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/sites/default/files/2023-01/Healthy-Soils-Are-full-of-life.pdf
3. USDA NRCS, Soil Health: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/conservation-basics/natural-resource-concerns/soil/soil-health
4. Ohio State University Extension, Understanding Soil Microbes and Nutrient Recycling: https://ohioline.osu.edu/factsheet/SAG-16
5. NC State Extension, Soil Biological Health: https://soilmanagement.ces.ncsu.edu/soil-health/soil-biological-health/
6. USDA NRCS, Biological Indicators and Soil Functions: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/sites/default/files/2023-01/Indicators-Biological-Indicators-and-Soil-Functions.pdf

sources.title: Healthy Soils Are Full of Life
Understanding Soil Microbes and Nutrient Recycling

scottyQuotes: There is a life web within the soil. It starts with bacteria and fungi and all these very small organisms that are living within the soil.
Living microbe-rich soil will have aggregates, this is a light glue layer that holds soil particles together.
The easiest way to show this is to find an aggregate and point out the difference.
